# CDC Partners Estimate Healthcare Cost of Antimicrobial-resistant Infections

## Key points

![Antibiotic Resistance Threats Cost 4.6 Billion Dollars Annually](/antimicrobial-resistance/media/images/Antibiotic-Resistance-Threats-cost-thump.jpg "Antibiotic Resistance Threats Cost 4.6 Billion Dollars Annually")

## CDC partners with experts to estimate healthcare costs of antimicrobial-resistant infection

**Posted: April 2021**

Each year, more than 35,000 people die because of antimicrobial-resistant infections, with more than 2.8 million infections occurring in the United States, according to CDC's *[Antibiotic Resistance Threats in the United States, 2019](/antimicrobial-resistance/media/pdfs/2019-ar-threats-report-508.pdf)* (2019 AR Threats Report). Many of the germs that cause these infections are frequently found in healthcare settings, resulting in increased risks to patient safety and a burden on the healthcare system as these infections often require substantial healthcare resources to treat.

To better understand the financial burden these infections place on health care, CDC teamed up with experts at the University of Utah School of Medicine and [released estimates](https://academic.oup.com/cid/article/72/Supplement_1/S17/6123350) that treating six of the most alarming antimicrobial resistance threats identified by CDC contribute to more than $4.6 billion in healthcare costs annually.

This is one of the largest studies on healthcare costs associated with treating antimicrobial-resistant infections to date, encompassing the total costs of medical personnel, equipment and the space necessary to treat these patients. The estimates do not include other downstream healthcare costs that occur after the first hospitalization or economic impacts to the patients, indicating that the overall costs could be even higher.

Leveraging the existing partnership through CDC's Prevention Epicenters Program—and resources from the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) Medical Center and the VA Health Services Research and Development Service—the researchers used methods designed to overcome limitations of previous studies to generate more accurate healthcare costs from AR.

![6 of the 18 most alarming antibiotic resistance threats cost the U.S. more than 4.6 billion dollars annually. Those include Vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus, Carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter species, Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus, Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales, Extended-spectrum cephalosporin resistance in Enterobacterales, and Multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa.](/antimicrobial-resistance/media/images/Antibiotic-Resistance-Threats-cost-thump.jpg "Antibiotic Resistance Threats Cost 4.6 Billion Dollars Annually")

The large size of the VA datasets and the detailed information they contained allowed the researchers to isolate cost information specifically attributable to the antimicrobial-resistant germs of interest while accounting for the location of the infection in the body and timing of infection while hospitalized. They then multiplied the pathogen-specific cost data by national infection case counts, previously published in the 2019 AR Threats Report and [Multidrug-Resistant Bacterial Infections in U.S. Hospitalized Patients, 2012-2017](https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1914433) to find the estimated total healthcare costs to treat these infections.

Cost estimates like these can help the U.S. Government, healthcare facilities and other organizations better understand the value of investments to prevent infections and slow the spread of germs. Estimates like these also underscore the healthcare costs associated with these infections and the economic benefits of preventing infections before they start.

CDC has proven that dedicated prevention and infection control efforts are working and are a best buy for public health. Between 2013-2019, investments in these areas have reduced deaths from antimicrobial resistance by 18% overall and nearly 30% in hospitals alone.

The University of Utah School of Medicine has partnered with CDC through its [Prevention Epicenters Program](/healthcare-associated-infections/php/prevention-epicenters/utah.html) and [Modeling Infectious Diseases in Healthcare (MInD)](/healthcare-associated-infections/php/research/mind-healthcare.html)—innovative research programs with academic investigators to conduct infection control and prevention research—since 2015, collaborating with the VA and its medical centers to explore and implement new prevention strategies and reduce antimicrobial resistance transmission (spread) in health care.

Partnerships like this one help CDC gain a better understanding of the impact of antimicrobial resistance in health care, help clinicians target infection control practices and support healthcare decision makers to make informed choices that improve patient safety.

Learn more about how CDC is [collaborating on research](/antimicrobial-resistance/media/pdfs/CDC-Fights-AR-Innovations-508.pdf) with partners, including the Prevention Epicenters Program and MInD, to help drive action to combat antimicrobial resistance and save lives.

## [Antimicrobial Resistance](/antimicrobial-resistance/index.html)

Antimicrobial resistance happens when germs like bacteria and fungi can defeat the drugs designed to kill them.

![Resistell logo](/images/Resistell-logo.png)

# Uncovering the hidden cost of delayed antibiotic resistance results

## Immediate financial and clinical impact of Antibiotic Resistance

Antibiotic resistance changes treatable infections into significant health threats. Along with the clinical challenges comes an extra financial load, as antibiotic resistance [typically adds around $1,400 to the hospital bill](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6929930/) for treating bacterial infections, according to the CDC in the US. This steep riseindicates a concerning trend where healthcare costs climb hand-in-hand with the complexity of treating resistant strains.

Patients face heftier medical bills, and healthcare providers deal with allocating limited resources. Increased financial stress on hospitals may lead to higher costs for all patients as healthcare facilities try to balance their budgets.

## Critical nature of Ultra-rapid Antibiotic Resistance Testing (AST)

The key to combating these infections is the speed at which patients receive the right antibiotics. Studies show that mortality rates drop significantly when patients are treated with appropriate therapy promptly. Ultra-rapid AST is essential for improving patient outcomes and reducing the overall effect of antibiotic resistance.

By ensuring effective treatment is delivered rapidly, not only do we enhance the recovery chances for patients, but we also lessen the spread and impact of antibiotic resistance. The link between rapid treatment and decreased mortality underscores the importance of healthcare systems prioritizing and improving their testing and treatment protocols.

## Consequences of treatment delays

The fallout from treatment delays reaches beyond the initial hospital bill. Patients who wait longer for the correct therapy are 31% less likely to return home after treatment, and their risk of in-hospital death or discharge to hospice increases by 16%. These figures from a recent [study](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0002962918304245) illustrate the cascade of negative outcomes that can stem from initial treatment setbacks.

Such delays not only affect patient recovery but also place unnecessary strain on families and the healthcare system. The consequences can spiral into longer hospital stays, more extensive use of healthcare resources, propagation of infections, and the emotional and financial weight on patients and their families.

## Future economic impact of Antimicrobial Resistance

Looking forward, the financial impact of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is daunting, with projections of an added [US$ 1 trillion in healthcare costs by 2050](https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/antimicrobial-resistance) if trends persist. This alarming forecastmirrors the critical need to tackle AMR on both economic and healthcare fronts. Without effective action, antibiotic resistance could undo medical advancements, turning minor infections into potential killers.

Hospitals are already spending an average of [USD 10,000 to 40,000 more per patient to treat those with antibiotic-resistant infections](https://www.oecd.org/health/health-systems/AMR-Policy-Insights-November2016.pdf) in OECD countries. These additional costs are a heavy burden on healthcare systems, taking funds away from other crucial areas and possibly resulting in reduced services or staffing. Controlling these expenses depends on our commitment to fighting antibiotic resistance with innovative strategies and strong policies.

## The strain on hospital resources

Hospital resources are further strained by the increased lengths of hospital stays when dealing with antibiotic-resistant microbes (ARMs). A study in Australia showed that [1079 antibiotic-resistant infections led to 12,138 extra days of hospitalization](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9045950/). These additional days not only have significant financial implications but also highlight the lost opportunity to treat other patients.

Hospitals must carefully manage their resources, as they work to provide extended treatment for patients with ARMs while still meeting the needs of others seeking care. The longer hospitalizations associated with ARMs create a range of logistical challenges, affecting everything from bed availability to the scheduling of surgeries and other procedures. Addressing this issue requires a comprehensive approach, with an emphasis on quick testing and the implementation of effective treatment plans.

## Strategies for mitigating costs and improving patient outcomes

Proactive measures are essential in reducing the time to receive antibiotic resistance results. Investment in ultra-rapid diagnostic testing technologies is a critical step toward timely and accurate identification of resistant bacteria. These sophisticated diagnostic tools not only speed up the treatment process but also ensure that patients receive the most effective antibiotics for their infections. By using ultra-rapid tests, healthcare providers can make informed decisions more quickly, minimizing the improper use of antibiotics and helping to curb the spread of resistance.

Antimicrobial stewardship programs form another vital part of the strategy, aiming to optimize antibiotic use across healthcare settings. These initiatives engage a team of healthcare professionals to assess and improve prescribing practices. Through stewardship, hospitals can ensure antibiotics are utilized appropriately, reducing the likelihood of resistance development and dissemination.

## The need for innovative solutions

The pressing impact of delayed antibiotic resistance is clear, with significant consequences for patient survival, healthcare quality, and the efficiency of our healthcare systems. The case studies and data underscore the vital need for timely and appropriate therapy to improve patient outcomes and alleviate the economic burden of antibiotic resistance.

As the threat of antibiotic-resistant infections continues to rise, the importance of rapid diagnostic tools and robust antimicrobial stewardship programs has never been more apparent. Here, the Resistell Multistar platform emerges as a critical solution, offering the fastest Antibiotic Susceptibility Testing (AST) available, with results in just 2 hours, by applying cutting-edge nanomotion technology.

Integrating Resistell Multistar into our healthcare strategy, can ensure quicker, more accurate treatment decisions, minimizing improper antibiotic use and combating the spread of resistance. Investing in such innovative technologies is not just about enhancing care for individual patients—it represents a forward-thinking approach to safeguarding public health and ensuring the sustainability of our healthcare systems. Learn more about Resistell Multistar.

The challenge of combating antibiotic resistance is multifaceted, but with ongoing innovation and adherence to best practices, we can reduce the risks and costs associated with delayed antibiotic resistance results. The future of healthcare may well hinge on our ability to respond effectively to the increasing threat of AMR.

![Uncovering the hidden cost of delayed antibiotic resistance results](/images/uncovering-the-hidden-cost-of-delayed-antibiotic-resistance-results.jpg)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Antimicrobial resistance leads to more severe and prolonged infections, resulting in higher rates of illness, complications, and a greater burden on healthcare systems.
Resistance contributes to increased fatalities, with over 35,000 deaths annually in the U.S. alone, underlining the critical danger posed by resistant infections.
Patients with resistant infections often face extended hospitalizations, which can result in thousands of extra patient days and strain healthcare resources significantly.
AMR adds approximately $66 billion to U.S. healthcare costs annually, with projections reaching $159 billion if current trends continue, stressing the economic impact.
